items.q: Why does the colour of a transition metal complex change when ligands are exchanged?
items.correct: Different ligands cause different amounts of d-orbital splitting, so different wavelengths of light are absorbed.
items.misconception: The colour changes because the metal ion itself changes oxidation state.
items.random_wrong: The colour changes because the new ligand is itself coloured.
items.why: Students assume colour comes from the ligand or from a redox change; they miss that the same metal ion gives different colours depending on which ligands are bound.

items.q: How is a dynamic equilibrium different from a static one?
items.correct: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; in a static equilibrium, nothing is happening at the particle level.
items.misconception: Dynamic and static equilibria are the same thing because both show constant macroscopic properties.
items.random_wrong: A dynamic equilibrium means the system is still changing visibly.
items.why: Macroscopically both look identical (no observable change), so students don't grasp the crucial particle-level distinction.

items.q: What does an increase in entropy of a system correspond to?
items.correct: An increase in the number of ways energy and particles can be arranged — typically a more dispersed/'disordered' state.
items.misconception: Entropy is just a measure of how messy a system looks visually.
items.random_wrong: Entropy measures how much energy a system contains in total.
items.why: The 'disorder' analogy is overused: students take it literally (mess vs tidy) instead of as a count of microstates.

items.q: Why can gases be compressed easily?
items.correct: There is empty space between gas particles
items.why: It's There is empty space between gas particles. “Gas particles are sticky” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which state is usually the least dense?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gas. “Solid” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A gas in a 1000 cm3 syringe is compressed to 250 cm3. By what factor was the volume reduced?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
